title: Hubbard Hall — stakeholder analysis
text:
1 Headline findings
This book explores Hubbard Hall’s stakeholders — donors, patrons, and class-takers. All charts are aggregates; no individual is identified. Data sources, definitions, and methods are in the Methodology appendix.
Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.viz import style, CATEGORICAL, BLUE, SEQUENTIAL_BLUE, INK, MUTED

households = read_parquet("processed", "households_summary.parquet")
regs = read_parquet("processed", "registrations_enriched.parquet")

# headline figures (computed, not hardcoded)
_order = ["[0, 1)", "[1, 5)", "[5, 10)", "[10, 20)", "[20, ∞)"]
_d = (
    households[households["is_donor"]]
    .groupby("distance_band", observed=True)["total_donations"]
    .sum()
    .reindex(_order)
    .fillna(0)
)
_c = _d.cumsum() / _d.sum()
within5, within10, within20 = (
    f"{_c.loc[b]:.0%}" for b in ("[1, 5)", "[5, 10)", "[10, 20)")
)
n_hh = f"{len(households):,}"
n_donors = f"{int(households['is_donor'].sum()):,}"
n_patrons = f"{int(households['is_patron'].sum()):,}"
n_class = f"{int(households['is_class_taker'].sum()):,}"
n_donor_class = f"{int((households['is_donor'] & households['is_class_taker']).sum()):,}"
n_all3 = f"{int((households['is_donor'] & households['is_patron'] & households['is_class_taker']).sum()):,}"

from hh.analytics.productions import is_excluded, count_succeeded_attendees

_near = ["[0, 1)", "[1, 5)", "[5, 10)"]
_cl = regs[regs["event_majorcat"] == "class"].dropna(subset=["distance_band"])
class_w10 = f"{_cl['distance_band'].isin(_near).mean():.0%}"
_th = regs[regs["event_minorcat"] == "theater"].dropna(subset=["distance_band"])
theater_w10 = f"{_th['distance_band'].isin(_near).mean():.0%}"
_perf = regs[(regs["event_majorcat"] == "performance") & ~regs["event_name"].map(is_excluded)].copy()
_perf["attendees"] = _perf["tickets"].map(count_succeeded_attendees)
_don = _perf.merge(households[["id", "is_donor"]], on="id", how="left")
donor_att_share = f"{_don.loc[_don['is_donor'].fillna(False), 'attendees'].sum() / _don['attendees'].sum():.0%}"

# donor concentration & persistence (new donor chapters)
from hh.clean.accounts import clean_accounts
from hh.clean.donations import clean_donations
from hh.analytics.donors import annual_gifts, donor_retention

_dt = households.loc[households["is_donor"], "total_donations"]
large_donor_share = f"{(_dt >= 1000).mean():.0%}"
large_dollar_share = f"{_dt[_dt >= 1000].sum() / _dt.sum():.0%}"

_ag = annual_gifts(clean_donations(accounts=clean_accounts()))
_max_year = int(_ag["year"].max())
_ret = donor_retention(_ag)
_ret = _ret[_ret["year"] < _max_year - 1]   # drop the row whose next year is the partial current year
_ret_year = int(_ret["year"].iloc[-1])
_recent_ret = f"{_ret['retention'].iloc[-1]:.0%}"

title: Hubbard Hall — stakeholder analysis
section: 1.1 Sunday is becoming the best-attended day
text:
1.1 Sunday is becoming the best-attended day
Within a theater run that plays Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, Saturday has long looked busiest — but mainly because it usually stages two shows. Per performance the three days are essentially tied, and since 2021 Sunday has drawn the most attendees per show in nearly every full-weekend run. Details in When do people come to Hubbard Hall?.

title: 2  Where do donors live?
text:
2.1 Has donor geography changed over time?
How far do Hubbard Hall’s donors live from the hall? Distances are straight-line (haversine) from the venue (25 E Main St, Cambridge NY) to each donor household, bucketed into half-open mile bands. Donors whose only address is a PO box are placed at their ZIP’s centroid and flagged “approximate” (excluded from the tight 1-mile count so it stays honest).
The tightest band includes some approximate (ZIP-centroid) placements — its centroid share, to read the 1-mile count in context:
The same dollars, split into roughly five-year periods and shown as shares within each period, so changes in the mix are visible regardless of overall growth. (This uses gift-level dates, unlike the lifetime household totals above.)
Code
from hh.clean.accounts import clean_accounts
from hh.clean.donations import clean_donations
from hh.analytics.donors import succeeded_individual_gifts

PERIODS = ["2013–2015", "2016–2020", "2021–2026"]
BANDS = ["[0, 1)", "[1, 5)", "[5, 10)", "[10, 20)", "[20, ∞)"]
BAND_COLORS = dict(zip(BANDS, ["#b7d3f6", "#6da7ec", "#3987e5", "#184f95", "#0d366b"]))

accounts_geo = read_parquet("processed", "accounts_geocoded.parquet")
gifts = succeeded_individual_gifts(clean_donations(accounts=clean_accounts()))
gifts["distance_band"] = gifts["account_id"].map(
    accounts_geo.set_index("account_id")["distance_band"]
)
gifts["period"] = pd.cut(
    gifts["donation_date"].dt.year, bins=[2010, 2015, 2020, 2026], labels=PERIODS
)
gd = (
    gifts.dropna(subset=["distance_band", "period"])
    .groupby(["period", "distance_band"], observed=True)["donation_amount"]
    .sum()
    .reset_index()
)
gd["share"] = gd.groupby("period", observed=True)["donation_amount"].transform(
    lambda s: s / s.sum()
)
w10 = {
    p: f"{gd[(gd['period'] == p) & (gd['distance_band'].isin(BANDS[:3]))]['share'].sum():.0%}"
    for p in PERIODS
}
Code
fig = px.bar(
    gd, x="period", y="share", color="distance_band",
    color_discrete_map=BAND_COLORS, category_orders={"distance_band": BANDS},
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Period", yaxis_title="Share of donated $", legend_title="Distance band"
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 2.2: Donor-dollar share by distance band, by period
The within-10-mile share of donated dollars was 54% in 2013–2015, 54% in 2016–2020, and 51% in 2021–2026.

title: 3  How has giving by donation size changed over time?
text:
3.1 Donors and dollars by tier, by year
Each household’s giving in a given year is put into one of four size tiers — <$100, $100–999, $1,000–4,999, and $5,000+ — so we can watch the mix of small and large donors shift over time. Giving is counted the same way as elsewhere: SUCCEEDED individual gifts (DONATION and PLEDGEPAYMENT), rolled up to the household (see Methodology). Donors are defined in Are large donors going away?; where they live is in Where do donors live?.
Code
fig = px.bar(
    plot_df,
    x="year",
    y="donors",
    color="tier",
    category_orders={"tier": TIER_LABELS, "year": complete_years},
    color_discrete_map=COLOR_MAP,
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Year (gift date)", yaxis_title="Donor households",
    legend_title="Annual giving", legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.2),
)
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:,.0f} donor-years<br>%{fullData.name}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 3.1: Active donor households by annual giving tier, by year
Code
fig = px.bar(
    plot_df,
    x="year",
    y="dollars",
    color="tier",
    category_orders={"tier": TIER_LABELS, "year": complete_years},
    color_discrete_map=COLOR_MAP,
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Year (gift date)", yaxis_title="Donated dollars",
    legend_title="Annual giving", legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.2),
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickprefix="$", tickformat=",.0f")
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:$,.0f}<br>%{fullData.name}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 3.2: Donated dollars by giving tier, by year
The small-donor base (under $100 a year) has grown sharply in recent years, while the number of households giving $1,000 or more in a year is small and uneven — its dollars, though, move the total most. The share of dollars coming from the top tiers:
Code
share = plot_df.assign(share=plot_df.groupby("year")["dollars"].transform(lambda s: s / s.sum()))
fig = px.bar(
    share, x="year", y="share", color="tier",
    category_orders={"tier": TIER_LABELS, "year": complete_years},
    color_discrete_map=COLOR_MAP,
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Year (gift date)", yaxis_title="Share of donated dollars",
    legend_title="Annual giving", legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.2),
    barmode="relative",
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%", range=[0, 1])
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:.0%}<br>%{fullData.name}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 3.3: Share of donated dollars by giving tier, by year

crumbs: 3  How has giving by donation size changed over time?

title: 4  Are large donors going away?
text:
4.1 Large donors active each year
“Large” here means a household that gave $1,000 or more in a single year. This chapter tracks how many such households are active each year, how many donors come back year to year, and how many once-active donors have gone quiet. Definitions and the retention method are in Methodology; the size tiers are introduced in How has giving by donation size changed over time?.
Code
fig = px.line(big_plot, x="year", y="donors", markers=True)
fig.update_traces(line_color=BLUE, marker_color=BLUE, hovertemplate="%{x}: %{y} households<extra></extra>")
fig.update_layout(xaxis_title="Year (gift date)", yaxis_title="Households giving $1,000+/yr")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 4.1: Households giving $1,000 or more in a year
crumbs: 4  Are large donors going away?

title: 4  Are large donors going away?
text:
4.2 Do donors come back?
Of the donors active in one year, the share who also gave the next:


Code
fig = px.line(ret_plot, x="year", y="retention", markers=True)
fig.update_traces(line_color=BLUE, marker_color=BLUE, hovertemplate="%{x}: %{y:.0%} returned<extra></extra>")
fig.update_layout(xaxis_title="Year (gave in …)", yaxis_title="Share who gave again next year")
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%", range=[0, 1])
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 4.2: Year-over-year donor retention
crumbs: 4  Are large donors going away?

title: 4  Are large donors going away?
text:
4.3 Who has gone quiet?
Of the 1,298 households that have ever given, 794 have not given in either of the last two complete years (2024 or 2025). Of those lapsed households, 69 were once large donors (gave $1,000+ in a year at some point). By lifetime giving tier:


Code
ldf = lapsed_by_tier.reset_index()
ldf.columns = ["Lifetime giving tier", "Lapsed households"]
ldf["Share of lapsed"] = ldf["Lapsed households"] / n_lapsed
ldf.style.format({"Lapsed households": "{:,.0f}", "Share of lapsed": pct})

2026 is year-to-date, so it is left out of the trends. So far in 2026, 4 households have reached the $1,000+ threshold; the full-year count will be higher. Retention is an upper bound on voluntary churn — a donor who has died also appears as “not retained.”
crumbs: 4  Are large donors going away?

title: 5  What do large donors attend?
text:
5.1 How engaged is each donor tier?
Donors are split by lifetime giving — smaller donor (under $1,000 lifetime), large donor ($1,000–4,999), and major donor ($5,000+) — and compared with households that have never given. The question is whether larger donors engage differently: do they attend more, take more classes, and prefer different performance genres? Attendance is headcount (succeeded ticket attendees); see Methodology. Size tiers are defined in How has giving by donation size changed over time?; the binary donor view is in What performances do donors attend?.
Code
eng = (
    hh.groupby("lt_group")
    .agg(
        households=("id", "count"),
        patron_share=("is_patron", "mean"),
        class_share=("is_class_taker", "mean"),
        avg_perf_regs=("n_performance", "mean"),
        avg_class_regs=("n_class", "mean"),
    )
    .reindex(GROUP_ORDER)
)
eng_show = eng.copy()
eng_show.columns = ["Households", "Attend performances", "Take classes", "Avg performance regs", "Avg class regs"]
eng_show.style.format(
    {
        "Households": "{:,.0f}",
        "Attend performances": pct,
        "Take classes": pct,
        "Avg performance regs": "{:.1f}",
        "Avg class regs": "{:.1f}",
    }
)

    eng[["patron_share", "class_share"]]
    .rename(columns={"patron_share": "Attend performances", "class_share": "Take classes"})
    .reset_index()
    .melt("lt_group", var_name="Activity", value_name="share")

fig = px.bar(
    eng_long, x="lt_group", y="share", color="Activity", barmode="group",
    category_orders={"lt_group": GROUP_ORDER},

    xaxis_title="", yaxis_title="Share of households", legend_title="",
    legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.25),

crumbs: 5  What do large donors attend?

title: 5  What do large donors attend?
section: 5.2 Which performances draw the large-donor tiers?
text:
5.2 Which performances draw the large-donor tiers?
Performance headcount by genre, split by lifetime-donor tier:


Code
m = regs.merge(hh[["id", "lt_group"]], on="id", how="left")
perf = m[(m["event_majorcat"] == "performance") & ~m["event_name"].map(is_excluded)].copy()
perf["attendees"] = perf["tickets"].map(count_succeeded_attendees)

gxg = perf.groupby(["lt_group", "event_minorcat"])["attendees"].sum().unstack(fill_value=0)
gxg = gxg.reindex(columns=GENRES).fillna(0)
genre_mix = gxg.div(gxg.sum(axis=1), axis=0).reindex(GROUP_ORDER)




Code
mix_long = genre_mix.reset_index().melt("lt_group", var_name="genre", value_name="share")
fig = px.bar(
    mix_long, x="lt_group", y="share", color="genre",
    category_orders={"lt_group": GROUP_ORDER, "genre": GENRES},
    color_discrete_map=GENRE_COLORS,
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="", yaxis_title="Share of performance attendance",
    legend_title="Genre", legend=dict(orientation="h", y=-0.25),
    barmode="relative",
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickformat=".0%", range=[0, 1])
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:.0%} %{fullData.name}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 5.2: Performance-genre mix within each lifetime-donor tier





Major donors are the most likely to attend performances (92% of them are patrons) and the most likely to take classes (65%). The genres they attend mirror the rest of the audience — theater first — but opera and music take a noticeably larger share of their attendance than of non-donors’.
crumbs: 5  What do large donors attend?

title: 6  What performances do donors attend?
text:
6.1 Which offerings drew the most donor attendance?
The overlap chapter showed that most donor households also attend; this one asks what they attend. Each registration is tagged with whether the registering household has ever given a SUCCEEDED individual gift (“donor household”). Performance attendance is headcount (succeeded ticket attendees — see Methodology), attributed to the registering household; canceled, moved, and rehearsal events are excluded.
Donor households account for 68% of all performance attendance. By genre:
Donor households are the majority of every genre’s audience — highest for music (77%), lowest for dance (62%), with theater at 64%. Selling tickets and cultivating donors are not separate audiences at Hubbard Hall.
Attendance from donor households by offering — production titles where the run mapping applies (recurring titles such as Winter Carnival aggregate across their annual runs), otherwise the event’s own name:
Code
perf["offering"] = perf["event_name"].map(lambda n: match_production(n) or n)
g = perf.groupby("offering").agg(
    genre=("event_minorcat", "first"),
    first_year=("starts_on", lambda s: int(s.dt.year.min())),
    attendees=("attendees", "sum"),
)
g["donor attendees"] = perf[perf["is_donor"]].groupby("offering")["attendees"].sum()
g["donor attendees"] = g["donor attendees"].fillna(0).astype(int)
g["donor share"] = g["donor attendees"] / g["attendees"]
top = g.sort_values("donor attendees", ascending=False).head(15)
top.index.name = None
top.style.format(
    {"attendees": "{:,}", "donor attendees": "{:,}", "donor share": pct}
)

The list is led by the big ensemble musicals and the summer operas — the same shows that fill the house generally — plus Winter Carnival of New Work, whose audience is the most donor-heavy of any large offering.
Do donor and non-donor households split their activity differently? Registration mix (all event types, registrations rather than headcount):
Code
mix = (
    m.groupby([m["is_donor"].map({True: "Donor households", False: "Non-donor households"}),
               "event_majorcat"], observed=True)
    .size()
    .unstack("event_majorcat")
    .fillna(0)
)
mix = mix.div(mix.sum(axis=1), axis=0)
mix.index.name = None
mix.style.format("{:.0%}")

Non-donor households skew toward classes; donor households split more evenly between classes and performances.
crumbs: 6  What performances do donors attend?

title: 7  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?
text:
7.1 Within a theater run: Friday, Saturday, or Sunday?
When the same production plays Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, which day do people pick? This chapter leads with that within-run view — the cleanest read on audience day preference, since it holds the show constant — then asks how the answer has changed over time, whether opera and music runs behave the same way, and closes with the coarser all-events view. How runs are reconstructed from Neon events, what is excluded, and how headcount is counted are in the Methodology appendix.
Only theater production runs that played all three of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday — mostly mainstage shows running one to four weekends. Attendance is true headcount, not registrations.
Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.viz import style, BLUE, CATEGORICAL
from hh.analytics.timing import WEEKDAY_ORDER
from hh.analytics.productions import production_performances, full_weekend_runs, time_slot

regs = read_parquet("processed", "registrations_enriched.parquet")
perf = production_performances(regs, minorcats=("theater",))
fw = full_weekend_runs(perf)

day_order = [d for d in WEEKDAY_ORDER if d in set(fw["weekday"])]
day = (
    fw.groupby("weekday")
    .agg(performances=("swept_event_id", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reindex(day_order)
)
day["attendees per performance"] = day["attendees"] / day["performances"]
day["share of attendees"] = day["attendees"] / day["attendees"].sum()

n_runs = fw["production_run"].nunique()
yr_span = f"{fw['starts_on'].min():%Y}–{fw['starts_on'].max():%Y}"
sat_share = f"{day.loc['Saturday', 'share of attendees']:.0%}"
per_perf = {
    d: f"{day.loc[d, 'attendees per performance']:.0f}"
    for d in ("Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday")
}
Across the 34 qualifying production runs (2013–2026), 296 performances drew 10,704 attendees. Any weekday rows below Friday–Sunday are the rare midweek performances inside a qualifying run (Winter Carnival 2015 played two Thursdays):
Code
day.style.format(
    {
        "performances": "{:,}",
        "attendees": "{:,}",
        "attendees per performance": "{:.1f}",
        "share of attendees": "{:.1%}",
    }
)

attendees

128

Saturday brings in the most people (42% of attendees) — but only because most runs play Saturday twice (matinee + evening). Per performance the three days are essentially tied: Friday 37, Saturday 35, and Sunday 37 attendees per show.
How each production splits across the three days (runs with fewer than 30 Fri–Sun attendees are left off the chart but appear in the table below):
Code
wk = fw[fw["weekday"].isin(["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"])]
run_day = wk.groupby(["production_run", "weekday"], as_index=False)["attendees"].sum()
run_day["share"] = run_day["attendees"] / run_day.groupby("production_run")[
    "attendees"
].transform("sum")
order = fw.groupby("production_run")["starts_on"].min().sort_values().index.tolist()
run_totals = wk.groupby("production_run")["attendees"].sum()
charted = [r for r in order if run_totals[r] >= 30]
DAY_COLORS = {"Friday": CATEGORICAL[0], "Saturday": CATEGORICAL[1], "Sunday": CATEGORICAL[2]}

fig = px.bar(
    run_day[run_day["production_run"].isin(charted)],
    x="share",
    y="production_run",
    color="weekday",
    orientation="h",
    category_orders={
        "production_run": charted[::-1],
        "weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"],
    },
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    custom_data=["attendees"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="%{x:.0%} — %{customdata[0]:,} attendees<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
)
fig.update_layout(
    height=740,
    xaxis_tickformat=".0%",
    xaxis_title="Share of run's Fri–Sun attendees",
    yaxis_title="",
)
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 7.1: Share of each run’s Fri–Sun attendees by day — one bar per production run, oldest at top
The same runs in detail — attendees by day, with each run’s dates and performance count:
Code
by_day = (
    fw.pivot_table(
        index="production_run",
        columns="weekday",
        values="attendees",
        aggfunc="sum",
        fill_value=0,

    .reindex(columns=day_order)
)
by_day["Total"] = by_day.sum(axis=1)
meta = fw.groupby("production_run").agg(
    first=("starts_on", "min"), last=("starts_on", "max"), performances=("swept_event_id", "count")
)
detail = meta.join(by_day).reindex(order)
detail["first"] = detail["first"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
detail["last"] = detail["last"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
detail.index.name = None
detail = detail.rename(columns={"first": "First", "last": "Last", "performances": "Perfs"})
detail.style.format("{:,}", subset=[c for c in detail.columns if c not in ("First", "Last")])

Saturday’s totals hide one more split: most runs play a 2 pm matinee and an evening show that day. Splitting Saturday performances by time slot:
Code
sat = fw[fw["weekday"] == "Saturday"].assign(slot=lambda d: d["event_name"].map(time_slot))
slot = (
    sat.groupby("slot")
    .agg(performances=("swept_event_id", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reindex(["matinee", "evening"])
)
slot["attendees per performance"] = slot["attendees"] / slot["performances"]
mat_rate = f"{slot.loc['matinee', 'attendees per performance']:.0f}"
eve_rate = f"{slot.loc['evening', 'attendees per performance']:.0f}"
slot.style.format(
    {"performances": "{:,}", "attendees": "{:,}", "attendees per performance": "{:.1f}"}
)

Saturday evenings draw 39 attendees per show versus 29 for Saturday matinees — the weakest slot of the weekend. Sunday’s single 2 pm show (37 per show) draws as well as any slot, so the matinee habit is a Sunday habit, not a Saturday one.
crumbs: 7  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?

title: 7  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?
section: 7.2 Has the best day changed over time?
text:
7.2 Has the best day changed over time?
The numbers above pool 2013–2026, but the day ranking has not been stable. Still holding the product constant (full-weekend theater runs, attendees per performance so days with two shows don’t get double credit), here is the same question across roughly five-year periods.


Code
PERIODS = ["2013–2015", "2016–2020", "2021–2026"]

def period(year):
    return pd.cut(year, bins=[2010, 2015, 2020, 2026], labels=PERIODS)

run_rate = (
    wk.groupby(["production_run", "weekday"], as_index=False)
    .agg(performances=("attendees", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
)
run_rate = run_rate[run_rate["production_run"].isin(charted)].copy()
run_rate["rate"] = run_rate["attendees"] / run_rate["performances"]
run_rate["run_start"] = run_rate["production_run"].map(
    wk.groupby("production_run")["starts_on"].min()
)

recent = run_rate[run_rate["run_start"] >= "2021-01-01"]
best_day = recent.loc[recent.groupby("production_run")["rate"].idxmax(), "weekday"]
n_recent = recent["production_run"].nunique()
n_recent_sun = int((best_day == "Sunday").sum())

wk_periods = wk.assign(period=period(wk["starts_on"].dt.year))
pd_rate = (
    wk_periods.groupby(["period", "weekday"], observed=True)
    .agg(performances=("attendees", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reset_index()
)
pd_rate["rate"] = pd_rate["attendees"] / pd_rate["performances"]
rate_of = lambda p, d: pd_rate.set_index(["period", "weekday"])["rate"].loc[(p, d)]


One dot per run and day — each run’s attendees per performance on Fridays, Saturdays, and Sundays:


Code
fig = px.scatter(
    run_rate.sort_values("run_start"),
    x="run_start",
    y="rate",
    color="weekday",
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    category_orders={"weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]},
    hover_name="production_run",
    custom_data=["performances", "attendees"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate=(
        "<b>%{hovertext}</b><br>%{y:.0f} attendees/perf "
        "(%{customdata[1]:,} over %{customdata[0]} shows)<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
    )
)
for d in ("Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"):  # 5-run rolling average as a guide line
    s = run_rate[run_rate["weekday"] == d].sort_values("run_start")
    fig.add_scatter(
        x=s["run_start"],
        y=s["rate"].rolling(5, center=True, min_periods=3).mean(),
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=DAY_COLORS[d], width=1.5),
        opacity=0.6,
        showlegend=False,
        hoverinfo="skip",
    )
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Run opening date", yaxis_title="Attendees per performance", legend_title=""
)
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 7.2: Attendees per performance by day, one point per full-weekend theater run




Aggregated to periods:


Code
fig = px.bar(
    pd_rate,
    x="period",
    y="rate",
    color="weekday",
    barmode="group",
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    category_orders={"period": PERIODS, "weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]},
    custom_data=["performances"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="%{y:.1f} attendees/perf (%{customdata[0]} shows)<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Period", yaxis_title="Attendees per performance", legend_title=""
)
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 7.3: Attendees per performance by day and period, full-weekend theater runs only




Per-performance attendance has roughly doubled across the periods, and the day ranking has flipped. In 2013–2015 Friday was the strongest day (27 per show vs 24 on Sunday); in 2016–2020 the days were about even; since 2021 Sunday is clearly strongest — 66 per show vs about 50 on Friday and Saturday. Sunday has been the best-attended day in 9 of the 9 full-weekend runs since 2021.
Recent-period caveats: 2021–2026 rests on 9 runs (52 performances), and recent runs rarely schedule Saturday matinees, which used to pull Saturday’s average down — yet Sunday leads anyway.
crumbs: 7  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?

title: 7  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?
section: 7.3 Do opera and music runs behave the same way?
text:
7.3 Do opera and music runs behave the same way?
Music and opera get the same treatment: map event names to productions, keep runs that played all of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. Almost all music events are one-off concerts (Music from Salem plays single afternoons, cabarets play one or two nights), so nearly everything that qualifies is opera — the summer festival stagings of 2013–2017, which also played real midweek (Wednesday/Thursday) performances — plus one 2015 musical folktale with a theater-style four-weekend run. No over-time comparison is possible: the qualifying runs all fall in 2013–2017.


Code
mo_fw = full_weekend_runs(production_performances(regs, minorcats=("music", "opera")))

mo_day_order = [d for d in WEEKDAY_ORDER if d in set(mo_fw["weekday"])]
mo_day = (
    mo_fw.groupby("weekday")
    .agg(performances=("swept_event_id", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reindex(mo_day_order)
)
mo_day["attendees per performance"] = mo_day["attendees"] / mo_day["performances"]
mo_day["share of attendees"] = mo_day["attendees"] / mo_day["attendees"].sum()
mo_rate = {d: f"{mo_day.loc[d, 'attendees per performance']:.0f}" for d in mo_day_order}
n_mo_runs = mo_fw["production_run"].nunique()


The 9 qualifying runs, chronologically:


Code
mo_by_day = mo_fw.pivot_table(
    index="production_run",
    columns="weekday",
    values="attendees",
    aggfunc="sum",
    fill_value=0,
).reindex(columns=mo_day_order)
mo_by_day["Total"] = mo_by_day.sum(axis=1)
mo_meta = mo_fw.groupby("production_run").agg(
    first=("starts_on", "min"), last=("starts_on", "max"), performances=("swept_event_id", "count")
)
mo_order = mo_meta["first"].sort_values().index.tolist()
mo_detail = mo_meta.join(mo_by_day).reindex(mo_order)
mo_detail["first"] = mo_detail["first"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
mo_detail["last"] = mo_detail["last"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
mo_detail.index.name = None
mo_detail = mo_detail.rename(columns={"first": "First", "last": "Last", "performances": "Perfs"})
mo_detail.style.format(
    "{:,}", subset=[c for c in mo_detail.columns if c not in ("First", "Last")]
)

crumbs: 7  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?

title: 7  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?
text:
7.4 Which days are busiest overall?
The coarser view: every event of every kind, whether or not it was part of a run. Counts here are registrations (a proxy for headcount), and because most events are scheduled on the day the organizers chose, this mostly reflects what gets booked rather than audience preference — use the within-run sections above for that. It is kept for context.


Code
cat_order = ["theater", "music", "dance", "opera", "class"]
named = regs[regs["event_minorcat"].isin(cat_order)].assign(
    cat=lambda d: d["event_minorcat"]
)
combo = pd.concat([named, regs[regs["event_majorcat"] == "class"].assign(cat="class")])
combo = combo.dropna(subset=["weekday"])
tab = combo.groupby(["cat", "weekday"], observed=True).size().reset_index(name="attendance")
fig = px.bar(
    tab,
    x="weekday",
    y="attendance",
    facet_col="cat",
    category_orders={"cat": cat_order, "weekday": WEEKDAY_ORDER},
    color_discrete_sequence=[BLUE],
)
fig.update_layout(yaxis_title="Registrations")
fig.for_each_xaxis(lambda a: a.update(title=""))
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 7.4: Registrations by day of week — one panel per programming type




Theater peaks Saturday; music peaks Sunday. Classes spread across the week with a Friday peak.
How far people travel — including whether Friday theater audiences travel farther than Sunday’s — is in How far do people travel to Hubbard Hall?.
crumbs: 7  When do people come to Hubbard Hall?

title: 8  How far do people travel to Hubbard Hall?
text:
8.1 Do weekend theater audiences travel differently?
The donor-geography chapter asked where the money comes from; this one asks where the people come from. Counts are registrations by the registering household’s distance band (straight-line miles — see Methodology); registrations without a geocodable address (about 2%) are dropped.
Registrations by distance band and programming type:
Classes serve the immediate community — 76% of class registrations come from within 10 miles (dance is similar at 76%). Performances pull from a much wider region: only 54% of theater, 59% of music, and 52% of opera registrations are that local, and roughly a third of theater (30%) and opera (34%) registrations come from beyond 20 miles.
For theater only, registrations from each distance band on each day of the week — “do Friday/Saturday audiences travel farther than Sunday’s?”
Code
theater = regs[regs["event_minorcat"] == "theater"].dropna(subset=["weekday", "distance_band"])
day_band = (
    theater.groupby(["weekday", "distance_band"], observed=True)
    .size()
    .unstack("distance_band")
    .reindex(index=WEEKDAY_ORDER, columns=BANDS)
    .fillna(0)
    .astype(int)
)
far_day = (day_band[BANDS[-1]] / day_band.sum(axis=1))
t = day_band.copy()
t["beyond 20 mi"] = far_day
t.style.format("{:,}", subset=BANDS).format({"beyond 20 mi": pct})

Sunday draws the farthest-traveling theater audience: 37% of Sunday registrations come from beyond 20 miles, versus 25% on Friday and 29% on Saturday. Friday is the most local night. That fits the within-run finding in When do people come to Hubbard Hall?: the Sunday matinee is not a neighborhood habit — it is when out-of-area visitors come.
crumbs: 8  How far do people travel to Hubbard Hall?

title: 9  Who takes classes at Hubbard Hall?
text:
9.1 Where class-takers live
A first look at the class-taking stakeholder group — how many households they are, where they live, how class-taking has changed over time, and how much they overlap with donors and performance audiences. This is a starter chapter; further class questions (which classes, who returns, class-takers as future donors) are queued in the appendix. Distances are straight-line from the hall; see Methodology. How far class and performance audiences travel is compared in How far do people travel to Hubbard Hall?.
1,819 households — 40% of all households — have registered for a class. Of those, 629 also donate and 711 also attend performances (see How do giving and participation overlap?).
Code
fig = px.bar(band.reset_index().rename(columns={"distance_band": "band", "id": "households"}),
             x="band", y="households", category_orders={"band": BAND_ORDER},
             color_discrete_sequence=[BLUE])
fig.update_layout(xaxis_title="Distance from Hubbard Hall (miles)", yaxis_title="Class-taking households")
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:,.0f} households<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 9.1: Class-taking households by distance from Hubbard Hall
61% of class-taking households live within 10 miles of the hall — classes draw mostly from the immediate area, more locally than performances do.
crumbs: 9  Who takes classes at Hubbard Hall?

title: 9  Who takes classes at Hubbard Hall?
text:
9.2 Class registrations over time


Code
fig = px.bar(by_year.reset_index(), x="year", y="registrations",
             category_orders={"year": complete_years}, color_discrete_sequence=[BLUE])
fig.update_layout(xaxis_title="Year (class start date)", yaxis_title="Class registrations")
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{x}: %{y:,.0f} registrations<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 9.2: Class registrations by year





2026 is year-to-date, so it is left out of the trend. So far in 2026 there have been about 341 class registrations; the full-year total will be higher.
crumbs: 9  Who takes classes at Hubbard Hall?

title: 10  How do giving and participation overlap?
text:
Each household is flagged is_donor (gave a SUCCEEDED individual gift), is_patron (registered for a performance), and is_class_taker (registered for a class).


Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.viz import style, CATEGORICAL

hh = read_parquet("processed", "households_summary.parquet")
counts = (
    pd.Series(
        {
            "Donors": hh["is_donor"].sum(),
            "Patrons (performances)": hh["is_patron"].sum(),
            "Class-takers": hh["is_class_taker"].sum(),
            "Donor and class": (hh["is_donor"] & hh["is_class_taker"]).sum(),
            "Donor and patron": (hh["is_donor"] & hh["is_patron"]).sum(),
            "All three": (hh["is_donor"] & hh["is_patron"] & hh["is_class_taker"]).sum(),
        },
        name="households",
    )
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={"index": "group"})
)




Code
fig = px.bar(counts, x="group", y="households", color="group", color_discrete_sequence=CATEGORICAL)
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, xaxis_title="", yaxis_title="Households")
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 10.1: Households in each giving / participation group




Which performances the donor households actually attend is in What performances do donors attend?; how far donors live from the hall is in Where do donors live?.
crumbs: 10  How do giving and participation overlap?

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.1 Data and reproducibility
This book is built from data pulled directly from the Neon CRM API. Every figure is reproducible: data is extracted, cleaned, geocoded, and summarized by the hh package, then read from saved parquet tables in data/20_processed/. All numbers in the prose are computed from the data at render time, so they stay correct as the data is refreshed. Every API pull is recorded in a manifest (endpoint, filters, timestamps, row counts, file hashes, code commit), and raw extracts are immutable — see the project README for the full provenance story.
All published charts and tables are aggregates (counts and dollar totals by category, distance band, and day of week) — no individual is identified.

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.2 About the data
The raw data is pulled from the Neon CRM REST API (v2) and saved exactly as Neon returns it. There are four entities, and every record retrieved streams straight to disk — analysis never depends on the live API.

Accounts — every constituent: donors, ticket-buyers, and class-takers, as individuals and households. One row per Neon account. Carries identity (account id, account type, first / last / full / company name), household membership (household id and name), mailing address (street, city, state, ZIP, country), contact info (email, phone), and the deceased and do-not-contact flags used to filter people out.
Donations — every gift. One row per donation, tied to an account by account id. Carries amount, date, type (DONATION or PLEDBGEPAYMENT), status (e.g. SUCCEEDED), and fund / campaign / purpose / source. It carries no household id; that link is recovered during cleaning.
Events — every scheduled event: a single performance, class, or community happening. One row per Neon event. Carries the event name, Neon’s event category, start and end dates, capacity, and Neon’s attendee count. There is no field linking the nights of a multi-night run; runs are reconstructed later.
Registrations — every ticket / registration purchase, linking an account to an event. One row per registration, with nested ticket and attendee records (used to count true headcount). Carries the registrant’s account id, amount, date-time, and source / origin.



Code
import pandas as pd
from hh.io import load_raw

_raw = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "entity": ["accounts", "donations", "events", "registrations"],
        "what it is": [
            "Constituents — donors, patrons, class-takers (individuals & households)",
            "Gifts, one per donation",
            "Scheduled events — one performance, class, or community event",
            "Ticket/registration purchases linking an account to an event",
        ],
    }
)
_raw["records"] = [len(load_raw(e)) for e in _raw["entity"]]
_raw[["entity", "records", "what it is"]]

Record counts are computed from the latest saved pull at render time, so they stay correct as the data is refreshed. Format & storage. Each pull is written as newline-delimited JSON (.jsonl, one record per line) into an immutable, timestamped folder data/00_raw/neon/pull-<UTC>/, alongside a manifest.yaml that records the endpoint, filters, output fields, Neon-reported counts, timestamps, file SHA-256 hashes, and the code commit. Accounts, donations, and events are pulled in one pass (scripts/pull.py); registrations are gathered by a per-event sweep (scripts/sweep_registrations.py) because Neon serves them event-by-event. Downstream code always reads these saved files (hh.io.load_raw), never the live API.

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.3 Data cleaning
Cleaning turns the raw API records into standardized analytic tables (the hh.clean package). It is purely deterministic and re-runs from the saved raw files on every scripts/build.py, so the processed tables in data/20_processed/ can always be rebuilt.

Column standardization. Neon’s output-field names are mapped to short snake_case names. Each entity has an explicit field map (for example, Account ID → account_id, Donation Amount → donation_amount); any column not in the map is converted by vmap, a faithful port of the R project’s renaming function (hh.clean.standardize).
Type parsing. Dollar amounts are coerced to numeric and dates to datetimes, with malformed values turned into missing rather than crashing.
Flag conversion. The deceased and do-not-contact text fields ("Yes") become real booleans.
Household rollup. Every analytic record gets a canonical household id, name, and group (hh.clean.households.add_rollup): id is the household id when Neon assigns one, otherwise the account id; group is household, account, or other; name is the first non-empty of household name, full name, or company name.
Recovering links the API omits. Donations carry an account id but no household id, so household membership is recovered by joining the cleaned accounts on account id. Registrations likewise join accounts (for the household) and events (for the event’s category), and fall back to a swept event id when the registration’s own event id is missing.
Event categorization. Each event is classified into a major category (performance / class / community / other) and, for performances, a minor category (theater / music / dance / opera / other) by ordered rules ported from the R project. An event that matches no rule is flagged ERROR on purpose, so anything uncategorized surfaces instead of hiding. See Definitions for the categories themselves.
Manual overrides. Curated one-off corrections live in config/overrides.yaml, kept separate from code.
Geographic cleaning. Addresses are resolved to a point with a documented precision — a real street address first, then a household member’s street address for PO-box accounts, then a ZIP centroid as a last resort, and unmapped if nothing works (see Geography). PO-box-only households are flagged so the tight distance counts stay honest.

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.4 Definitions
How we select and define the people and events in this book. Every definition rolls a constituent up to their household first (see Data cleaning); person-level counts are at the household level.

Household. The analytic unit: the household id when Neon assigns one, otherwise the single account id. A person in a Neon household is counted once with their housemates; a standalone account is its own household.
Donor. A household whose total succeeded individual gifts are greater than zero. A gift counts when its status is SUCCEEDED, its type is DONATION or PLEDBGEPAYMENT, and the account is an Individual; we then drop gifts from accounts marked deceased or do-not-contact, and a known junk account (36805). Variables used: donation_amount, donation_status, donation_type, account_type, deceased, do_not_contact.
Patron. A household with one or more performance registrations (is_patron = n_performance > 0).
Class-taker. A household with one or more class registrations (is_class_taker = n_class > 0).
Major category (performance / class / community / other). Assigned from Neon’s event category and the event name, in order, first match wins. For example, an event in a Performances, Theater Performances, Music Performances, or Opera Performances category is a performance (with a few named exceptions that are really classes); events in class or community categories fall to those buckets; anything unmatched is other. An event matching no rule at all is flagged ERROR as a tripwire.
Minor category (theater / music / dance / opera / other — performances only). Assigned after the major category, again first match wins; anything unmatched is other. Theater and opera lean on Neon’s category plus title patterns; music and dance use category plus title patterns.

Theater — category Shakespeare or Theater Performances, or a title matching a known theater production.
Opera — category Opera Performances, or the title contains “Opera”.
Music — a Music category, or the title matches music patterns (Concert, Cabaret, named artists, and the like).
Dance — a Dance category or title.

Attendance (headcount). A registration is a purchase, not a person; it averages about two attendees. Headcount counts ticket attendees whose registration status is SUCCEEDED (see Attendance headcount).
Production and run. A production is a title matched from the event name to a curated list; a run is that title’s performances split when dates gap by more than 45 days (see Reconstructing multi-day production runs).

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.5 Donor size and persistence

Annual giving tiers. A household’s giving in a single year is placed in one of four bands: <$100, $100–999, $1,000–4,999, or $5,000+. These drive the over-time and persistence chapters. A household is counted once per year it gives (so a multi-year donor appears in several years).
Lifetime tiers. Cumulative giving uses the same bands; households giving $1,000+ lifetime are “large donors” and $5,000+ are “major donors.” These drive the preferences chapter, where lifetime giving measures the depth of the relationship.
Donor basis. Size and persistence start from succeeded_individual_gifts (status SUCCEEDED, type DONATION/PLEDBGEPAYMENT, account type Individual) rolled up to the household — the same basis as households_summary.total_donations, so the figures reconcile with the headline donor counts.
Retention. Of households that gave in year Y, the share that also gave in Y+1. The retention table drops the final year (it has no “next year”), and the trend drops the row whose next year is the partial current year. Retention is an upper bound on voluntary churn: a donor who has died also appears as “not retained.”
Partial years. The current calendar year is year-to-date and is left out of every over-time trend; where its figure is shown, it is labeled year-to-date and will rise as the year fills out.

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.6 Geography
Distances are straight-line (haversine) from Hubbard Hall (25 E Main St, Cambridge NY) to each household, bucketed into half-open mile bands. Households whose only address is a PO box are placed at their ZIP’s centroid and flagged “approximate”; they are excluded from the tight 1-mile count so it stays honest.

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.7 Attendance headcount
Registration counts understate attendance: one registration is a purchase, and averages about two attendees. Where the text says headcount, it counts ticket attendees whose registration status is SUCCEEDED — this also automatically drops canceled and refunded attendees.

title: Appendix A — Methodology
text:
A.8 Reconstructing multi-day production runs
Each performance night is a separate Neon event (e.g. “Fun Home - Friday, February 3 at 7:30 pm”) — there is no field linking the nights of a run. Runs are therefore reconstructed by a manually curated mapping from event names to productions (hh.analytics.productions):

Mapping. Every theater event name matches exactly one production title pattern (coverage is exhaustive and tested). For music and opera, only titles with multiple performances are mapped; one-off concerts cannot form a run and are dropped.
Runs. A production’s performances are split into separate runs when consecutive dates are more than 45 days apart — this separates recurring titles (Winter Carnival of New Work, Whispering Bones, Bread & Puppet) into their yearly runs.
Exclusions. Events that were not regular public performances are dropped before mapping: canceled or moved dates, test events, open/dress/pay-what-you-will rehearsals, online productions, day-long special events, and weekday 10 am school shows.
Full-weekend runs. The within-run day-of-week analysis keeps only runs that played all three of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, so day comparisons hold the production constant.
Time slots. A performance is classified a matinee if its name contains “2 pm” or “matinee”; otherwise evening.

title: Appendix B — Questions to answer next
text: A live snapshot of TODO.md at the repo root, kept in the appendix so the roadmap is always in front of us. Edit TODO.md and this page updates on the next render.

title: Appendix B — Questions to answer next
text:
B.1 Giving

Do class-takers and patrons become donors? First-activity date vs first-gift date per household: how often does participation precede giving, and by how long? (Feasible now: registered_at + donation_date.)
Who has lapsed? Donor households that gave in earlier years but not recently — recency/frequency segmentation, and how much annual giving the lapsed group used to represent. (Feasible now.)
How concentrated is giving? Share of lifetime dollars from the top 10 / 50 households; dependence on a few large gifts, and whether concentration is rising. (Feasible now; publish only aggregate shares, never named households.)
When during the year do donations arrive? Seasonality, year-end share, spikes around events or appeals. (Feasible now.)
Do donors who attend give more than donors who don’t? Giving levels by engagement tier (donate-only vs donate+attend vs all three). (Feasible now via households_summary.)

title: Appendix B — Questions to answer next
text:
B.2 Audience

How far in advance do people buy tickets? Lead time (registered_at to starts_on) by genre and day of week; the walk-up share. (Feasible; first check how much of registered_at is real timestamps vs batch-entered dates.)
Which shows bring in first-timers? For each production, how many attending households were brand new — and did they ever come back? Acquisition value of programming. (Feasible now; builds on the production mapping.)
How many attendees come back? Repeat rate and one-and-done share by genre; do class households attend more performances than performance-only households attend classes? (Feasible now.)
Has the audience gotten more or less local over time? Distance mix of registrations by period, overall and by genre — the audience analogue of the donor-geography over-time section. (Feasible now.)
Are sell-outs predictable? “SOLD OUT” in event names is a weak label; check whether the events extract includes a capacity field so utilization can be computed properly. (Needs a look at events.jsonl — possible data gap.)

title: Appendix B — Questions to answer next
text:
B.3 Data gaps to investigate

Event capacity (for utilization / sell-out analysis) — is it populated in Neon?
Memberships — if Hubbard Hall uses Neon’s membership module, pulling it would open “do members behave differently?” questions.
Campaign/appeal codes on donations — would let “when do donations arrive?” separate appeal-driven from spontaneous gifts.
